perturbation.original_text: For purposes of this Agreement, “Confidential Information” means any information with respect to the Disclosing Party that is not generally known to the public and was obtained by the Receiving Party as a result of the Opportunity.
perturbation.changed_text: For purposes of this Agreement, “Confidential Information” means any information with respect to the Disclosing Party, whether or not generally known to the public, and includes all information irrespective of how the Receiving Party obtained it.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition limits confidential information to that 'not generally known to the public' and 'obtained as a result of the Opportunity'. The modified version broadens this to include any information, regardless of public knowledge or how it was obtained. This creates a conflict because trade secret law, for instance, requires information to be secret to be protected.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA defines a trade secret as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known. The modified definition includes information 'whether or not generally known to the public', directly contradicting this fundamental requirement for trade secret protection.

Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The modified text broadens the definition of 'Confidential Information' to include information 'whether or not generally known to the public'. This directly contradicts the requirement for trade secret protection, as trade secrets must derive economic value from *not* being generally known, according to the provided Law Explanation. While the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the Law Explanation and the Perturbation Explanation clearly outline the contradiction, because legally protected trade secrets cannot be public knowledge. The modified clause would fail legal scrutiny because it attempts to protect information that does not meet the legal requirements for protection.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees that it will use the Confidential Information solely for the purpose of evaluating the Opportunity and will not use the Confidential Information for any other purpose.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party agrees that it will use the Confidential Information for its own benefit and any purposes it deems necessary, and will not be liable to the Disclosing Party for any damages.
perturbation.explanation: The original text restricts the use of Confidential Information to evaluating the Opportunity. The modified text allows the Receiving Party to use the information for any purpose, negating the confidentiality obligation and creating potential conflicts of interest. This contradicts the basic fiduciary duties implied in confidentiality agreements.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 395 of the Restatement (Second) of Agency states that an agent (here, the Receiving Party) has a duty not to use confidential information for their own benefit. The modified text explicitly allows the Receiving Party to use the information for their own benefit, directly contradicting this duty.
perturbation.location: Section regarding Use of Confidential Information

perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the fiduciary duties implied in confidentiality agreements, as explained. The original text restricted the use of Confidential Information to evaluating the Opportunity, while the changed text allows the Receiving Party to use the information for any purpose it deems necessary, thereby negating the confidentiality obligation. The Law Explanation also supports this, stating that the modified text explicitly allows the Receiving Party to use the information for their own benefit, contradicting the agent's duty not to use confidential information for their own benefit, according to Section 395 of the Restatement (Second) of Agency. Although Snippet 1 is missing and Snippet 2 is about the American Law Institute, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both indicate a contradiction and support a YES determination.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees to protect the Confidential Information by using the same degree of care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care, as the Receiving Party uses to protect its own confidential information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party agrees to make its best effort to protect the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates a 'reasonable degree of care'. The modified text only requires 'best effort'. 'Reasonable care' has a legal definition. 'Best effort' is less precise and offers weaker protection. This introduces ambiguity and weakens the standard of care expected.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1714(a) establishes a standard of 'reasonable care' in preventing harm. Replacing 'reasonable degree of care' with 'best effort' lowers the standard, potentially contradicting the legally recognized standard of care required in similar situations.
perturbation.location: Section regarding Protection of Confidential Information
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1714 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 3. OBLIGATIONS IMPOSED BY LAW [1708 - 1725] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) 1714. (a) Everyone is responsible, not only for the result of his or her willful acts, but also for an injury occasioned to another by his or her want of ordinary care or skill in the management of his or her property or person, except so far as the latter has, willfully or by want of ordinary care, brought the injury upon himself or herself. The design, distribution, or marketing of firearms and ammunition is not exempt from the duty to use ordinary care and skill that is required by this section. The extent of liability in these cases is defined by the Title on Compensatory Relief. (b) It is the intent of the Legislature to abrogate the holdings in cases such as Vesely v. Sager (1971) 5 Cal.3d 153, Bernhard v. Harrah’s Club (1976) 16 Cal.3d 313, and Coulter v. Superior Court (1978) 21 Cal.3d 144 and to reinstate the prior judicial interpretation of this section as it relates to proximate cause for injuries incurred as a result of furnishing alcoholic beverages to an intoxicated person, namely that the furnishing of alcoholic beverages is not the proximate cause of injuries result
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces the 'reasonable degree of care' standard with 'best effort.' California Civil Code § 1714(a) establishes a 'reasonable care' standard for preventing harm. The change weakens the legally recognized standard of care, creating a contradiction because 'best effort' is a less precise and potentially lower standard than 'reasonable care' as defined within the code. The original text mandated a specific level of care, aligning with the legal standard, while the modified text introduces ambiguity and a potentially weaker obligation.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York, without regard to its conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of Atlantis, without regard to its conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause specifies New York law. The modified clause specifies the laws of 'Atlantis,' a fictional place. This makes the governing law clause unenforceable and creates ambiguity. The agreement is now silent on the law which applies to it.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 187 deals with the law governing in the absence of an effective choice by the parties. By specifying a non-existent jurisdiction, the agreement fails to make an effective choice, potentially leading to disputes over which jurisdiction's laws actually apply and making the contract unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: § 1-301. Territorial Applicability; Parties' Power to Choose Applicable Law. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-301. Territorial Applicability; Parties' Power to Choose Applicable Law. a) Except as otherwise provided in this section, when a transaction bears a reasonable relation to this state and also to another state or nation the parties may agree that the law either of this state or of such other state or nation shall govern their rights and duties. (b) In the absence of an agreement effective under subsection (a), and except as provided in subsection (c), [the Uniform Commercial Code] applies to transactions bearing an appropriate relation to this state. (c) If one of the following provisions of [the Uniform Commercial Code] specifies the applicable law, that provision governs and a contrary agreement is effective only to the extent permitted by the law so specified: (1) Section 2-402 ; (2) Sections 2A-105 and 2A-106 ; (3) Section 4-102 ; (4) Section 4A-507 ; (5) Section 5-116 ; [(6) Section 6-103 ; (7) Section 8-110 ; (8) Sections 9-301 through 9 - 307 . Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a legal contradiction by specifying the laws of 'Atlantis,' a fictional place, to govern the agreement. This renders the governing law clause ineffective. Scraped Law Snippet 2, § 1-301 of the Uniform Commercial Code, addresses the parties' power to choose applicable law, stating that parties may agree that the law of a state or nation shall govern their rights and duties. The changed text's reference to 'Atlantis' fails to meet the requirement of selecting a valid state or nation, creating ambiguity and undermining the enforceability of the governing law clause. Since the changed text fails to meet the condition of subsection (a) of Snippet 2, then subsection (b) applies, which says UCC applies to transactions bearing an appropriate relation to the state. By specifying a non-existent jurisdiction, the agreement fails to make an effective choice, as the law explanation suggests, leading to potential disputes and unenforceability.
